sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: Cue reactivity after nicotine-extinction training. Researchers examined whether extinction-based training reduces physiological cue reactivity among daily nicotine users using a design intended to connect a psychological or sociological construct with a measurable behavioral outcome. The study included 164 adult nicotine users assigned to cue exposure with or without response-prevention coaching and compared Assessment only, Cue exposure, Cue + prevention. The investigators selected these conditions because they differed in exposure to smoking cues and the addition of response-prevention coaching, while keeping the setting, instructions, and timing of the outcome assessment similar across groups. The primary outcome was self-reported craving after standardized cue presentation; a secondary measure was skin conductance response and latency to first lapse in a follow-up week. Participants also completed baseline questionnaires measuring nicotine dependence score and baseline craving, which allowed the investigators to check whether preexisting differences might explain the pattern of results. Figure 1 summarizes the main outcome. The overall pattern was that cue exposure reduced craving, and adding response-prevention coaching produced the lowest cue-reactivity score. The authors interpreted this pattern as evidence related to classical conditioning, extinction, and substance-use cue reactivity, but they cautioned that the study did not measure every possible mechanism. For example, expectancy effects or temporary demand characteristics during the laboratory session could also affect the outcome unless future work specifically controls or manipulates it. The investigators therefore described the results as supporting a particular interpretation rather than proving that a single mental state or social process fully explained every observed difference. The study also included a manipulation check. Participants in the condition most directly targeting classical conditioning, extinction, and substance-use cue reactivity reported greater change in craving rating and physiological arousal to cues than participants in the comparison condition, whereas baseline scores did not differ meaningfully across groups. The authors argued that this pattern increased confidence that the intervention changed the intended construct. However, they noted that self-report measures can be influenced by demand characteristics and that behavior in a study setting may not perfectly generalize to natural settings. In a follow-up analysis, the authors tested whether baseline nicotine dependence altered the size of the effect. This analysis was exploratory and was not powered to make strong subgroup claims. The authors suggested that a more rigorous next step would be testing spontaneous recovery and renewal by presenting cues in a different real-world context. The investigators emphasized that the variables were operational definitions of broader constructs rather than direct measurements of every psychological or social process that might be relevant. Participants completed baseline measures before randomization or grouping, and the investigators checked whether the comparison groups differed on prior exposure, demographic characteristics, and initial scores on the main outcome.
questions.stem: Which interpretation of Figure 1 is most consistent with the investigators' central claim?

choices.A: The outcome pattern supports the claim that changing classical conditioning, extinction, and substance-use cue reactivity can alter self-reported craving after standardized cue presentation in this setting, while still leaving some alternative mechanisms unresolved.
choices.B: The figure proves that expectancy effects or temporary demand characteristics during the laboratory session played no role, because any randomized or comparison-group design eliminates all unmeasured mechanisms.
choices.C: The study cannot support any inference about classical conditioning, extinction, and substance-use cue reactivity because psychological and social constructs cannot be operationalized with measurable outcomes.
choices.D: The safest conclusion is that the comparison group with the lowest mean performed best, because smaller bars or lower scores are always favorable in behavioral figures. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats classical conditioning, extinction, and substance-use cue reactivity as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.

explanations.B: This choice is tempting because it highlights a real issue in research on classical conditioning, extinction, and substance-use cue reactivity, but it does not fit the passage as well as the credited answer. The option says, 'The figure proves that expectancy effects or temporary demand characteristics during the laboratory session played no role, because any randomized or comparison-group design eliminates all unmeasured mechanisms.' Yet the study manipulated exposure to smoking cues and the addition of response-prevention coaching and measured self-reported craving after standardized cue presentation, so the stronger inference must connect those operational definitions to the figure. A student might pick this if they recognized a related term but ignored whether the design actually supports that specific claim. The passage leaves room for expectancy effects or temporary demand characteristics during the laboratory session, but that limitation does not reverse the observed pattern or erase the construct being tested.

Yet the study manipulated exposure to smoking cues and the addition of response-prevention coaching and measured self-reported craving after standardized cue presentation, so the stronger inference must connect those operational definitions to the figure. explanations.A: Correct. This answer best integrates the design, Figure 1, and the construct being tested. The passage says the groups differed in exposure to smoking cues and the addition of response-prevention coaching and that the primary outcome was self-reported craving after standardized cue presentation; the figure then shows that cue exposure reduced craving, and adding response-prevention coaching produced the lowest cue-reactivity score. That combination supports the inference in the answer without overstating it as proof of every possible mechanism. It also respects the distinction between a construct, such as classical conditioning, extinction, and substance-use cue reactivity, and the way that construct was operationalized through craving rating and physiological arousal to cues and the measured outcome. questions.stem: In this study, craving rating and physiological arousal to cues is best described as which type of variable?

choices.A: It is an operationalized measure of classical conditioning, extinction, and substance-use cue reactivity, meaning it is a concrete indicator used to study a broader theoretical construct.
choices.B: It is the independent variable only because all self-report measures are automatically assigned by the researcher rather than measured from participants.
choices.C: It is a biological confound that makes the social construct irrelevant, since subjective ratings can never be used in behavioral research.
choices.D: It is the sampling frame, because any questionnaire completed before the outcome determines which participants entered the study. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats classical conditioning, extinction, and substance-use cue reactivity as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.

questions.stem: Which additional finding would most strengthen the proposed mechanism rather than merely repeating the same outcome?

choices.A: Showing that every participant preferred the intervention would prove the mechanism, even if preference was unrelated to self-reported craving after standardized cue presentation.
choices.B: Showing that baseline scores differed strongly between groups would strengthen the causal interpretation by making the groups easier to separate.
choices.C: Removing the manipulation check would strengthen the study because fewer measures always reduce the risk of measurement bias. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats classical conditioning, extinction, and substance-use cue reactivity as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.
choices.D: Showing that changes in craving rating and physiological arousal to cues statistically mediate the effect of condition on self-reported craving after standardized cue presentation would better connect the manipulation to the proposed mechanism.

questions.stem: Which limitation should most temper the authors' conclusion about classical conditioning, extinction, and substance-use cue reactivity?

choices.A: The study is invalid because any use of a comparison condition prevents researchers from drawing conclusions about group differences. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats classical conditioning, extinction, and substance-use cue reactivity as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.
choices.B: The finding is impossible to interpret because a significant behavioral outcome can never coexist with an imperfect manipulation check.
choices.C: The design does not fully rule out expectancy effects or temporary demand characteristics during the laboratory session, so the result supports the authors' interpretation but does not uniquely identify one mechanism.
choices.D: The result must be due to random error because all social and psychological interventions produce identical effects across participants.

questions.stem: Which follow-up design would best test the generalizability of the result?

choices.A: Repeating the same analysis on the same participants without collecting new data would establish external validity because the sample size would appear larger.
choices.B: Dropping the comparison group would improve generalizability by making the design more similar to everyday life, even though it weakens interpretation. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats classical conditioning, extinction, and substance-use cue reactivity as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.
choices.C: Replicating the intervention in a different setting and measuring self-reported craving after standardized cue presentation over a longer follow-up would test whether the pattern extends beyond the original sample.
choices.D: Using only participants who responded most strongly would make the result more representative because it would maximize the size of the observed effect.

questions.passage: Sleep deprivation and alcohol-expectancy decisions. Researchers examined whether acute sleep deprivation changes risk appraisal in alcohol-related decision scenarios using a design intended to connect a psychological or sociological construct with a measurable behavioral outcome. The study included 132 young adults completing decision tasks after normal sleep or restricted sleep and compared Normal sleep, Restricted sleep, Restricted + nap. The investigators selected these conditions because they differed in sleep opportunity before the decision task and a brief recovery nap, while keeping the setting, instructions, and timing of the outcome assessment similar across groups. The primary outcome was rate of risky choices in alcohol-related scenarios; a secondary measure was subjective sleepiness and inhibitory-control accuracy. Participants also completed baseline questionnaires measuring usual sleep duration, sensation seeking, and prior alcohol use, which allowed the investigators to check whether preexisting differences might explain the pattern of results. Figure 1 summarizes the main outcome. The overall pattern was that sleep restriction increased risky choices, while a nap partially reduced the effect. The authors interpreted this pattern as evidence related to consciousness, sleep, inhibition, and decision making, but they cautioned that the study did not measure every possible mechanism. For example, differences in sensation seeking or prior alcohol experience could also affect the outcome unless future work specifically controls or manipulates it. The investigators therefore described the results as supporting a particular interpretation rather than proving that a single mental state or social process fully explained every observed difference. The study also included a manipulation check. Participants in the condition most directly targeting consciousness, sleep, inhibition, and decision making reported greater change in risk choices and inhibitory-control accuracy than participants in the comparison condition, whereas baseline scores did not differ meaningfully across groups. The authors argued that this pattern increased confidence that the intervention changed the intended construct. However, they noted that self-report measures can be influenced by demand characteristics and that behavior in a study setting may not perfectly generalize to natural settings. In a follow-up analysis, the authors tested whether baseline inhibitory-control score altered the size of the effect. This analysis was exploratory and was not powered to make strong subgroup claims. The authors suggested that a more rigorous next step would be measuring real-time executive-control activity or using a within-subject crossover design. The investigators emphasized that the variables were operational definitions of broader constructs rather than direct measurements of every psychological or social process that might be relevant. Participants completed baseline measures before randomization or grouping, and the investigators checked whether the comparison groups differed on prior exposure, demographic characteristics, and initial scores on the main outcome.

choices.A: The figure proves that differences in sensation seeking or prior alcohol experience played no role, because any randomized or comparison-group design eliminates all unmeasured mechanisms.

questions.stem: Which limitation should most temper the authors' conclusion about consciousness, sleep, inhibition, and decision making?

choices.A: The study is invalid because any use of a comparison condition prevents researchers from drawing conclusions about group differences. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats consciousness, sleep, inhibition, and decision making as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.
choices.B: The design does not fully rule out differences in sensation seeking or prior alcohol experience, so the result supports the authors' interpretation but does not uniquely identify one mechanism.
choices.C: The finding is impossible to interpret because a significant behavioral outcome can never coexist with an imperfect manipulation check.
choices.D: The result must be due to random error because all social and psychological interventions produce identical effects across participants.

questions.stem: Which follow-up design would best test the generalizability of the result?

choices.A: Replicating the intervention in a different setting and measuring rate of risky choices in alcohol-related scenarios over a longer follow-up would test whether the pattern extends beyond the original sample.
choices.B: Repeating the same analysis on the same participants without collecting new data would establish external validity because the sample size would appear larger.
choices.C: Dropping the comparison group would improve generalizability by making the design more similar to everyday life, even though it weakens interpretation. This would be a recognizable misconception because it treats consciousness, sleep, inhibition, and decision making as a label rather than linking it to the passage's actual measurement strategy.
choices.D: Using only participants who responded most strongly would make the result more representative because it would maximize the size of the observed effect.
